summary: Legendary Japanese film director (1910–1998), often called 'the Emperor' of cinema. Best known for Seven Samurai, Rashomon, Ran, Ikiru, High and Low, and Yojimbo. A defining figure of Golden Age Japanese cinema alongside Yasujiro Ozu and Kenji Mizoguchi. Long-term collaborator with actor Toshiro Mifune. His work heavily influenced Western cinema, especially via George Lucas and Sergio Leone.

works.name: Seven Samurai (1954)
works.description: Epic jidaigeki film in which a village of peasants hire seven samurai to defend them from bandits. The most frequently clued Kurosawa film in quizbowl. Remade as The Magnificent Seven (1960, John Sturges) and inspired A Fistful of Dollars. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Peasants hire seven samurai/warriors to protect their village from bandits
examples: In that film by him, which was given a Western adaptation called The Magnificent Seven, peasants hire the title soldiers as protection from bandits.
he showed a hired group of warriors defend a farming hamlet from a group of thugs in Seven Samurai

clues.clue: The film ends with two samurai dying to musket fire in a rainy battle; a surviving samurai says 'The farmers have won, not us'
examples: A film by this director ends with two of the title characters dying to musket fire in a rainy battle, after which another wistfully notes, 'The farmers have won, not us.'
The final shot of another film by that director with this surname shows swords implanted in four burial mounds as one character says 'The victory belongs to the peasants.'

clues.clue: Remade as The Magnificent Seven (1960) by John Sturges starring Yul Brynner
examples: A 1960 John Sturges film starring Yul Brynner was also based on one of this director's movies.
John Sturges' The Magnificent Seven was a remake of Kurosawa's film about seven warriors who help protect a village from bandits.

examples: This director's use of two peasants to frame one of his films was imitated by George Lucas' similar use of C-3P0 and R2-D2.
A film by one of these directors adapted two bickering peasants from a film by the other and turned them into robots

clues.clue: Toshiro Mifune played Kikuchiyo in Seven Samurai
examples: He played the title bodyguard of Yojimbo, and portrayed Kikuchiyo in Seven Samurai.

works.name: Rashomon (1950)
works.description: Landmark film presenting multiple contradictory accounts of a samurai's murder and his wife's rape, told by different witnesses. Ends with a woodcutter taking in an abandoned baby. Said to be the first Kurosawa film to aim the camera directly at the sun. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Various characters tell conflicting/different versions of a samurai's murder and the rape of his wife
examples: A woodcutter and a priest discuss different murder accounts in a film by a director with this surname
various characters tell conflicting versions of the story of the murder of a samurai and the rape of his wife

clues.clue: Ends with a woodcutter deciding to take care of an abandoned baby
examples: At the end of this film, a woodcutter decides to take care of an abandoned baby.
a 1950 film which ends with the sun coming out as an abandoned baby is taken home by the woodcutter, played by Takashi Shimura

clues.clue: Toshiro Mifune played the bandit Tajomaru in Rashomon
examples: This actor's more famous roles include the analogue to Macbeth in Throne of Blood and the bandit Tajomaru in Rashomon.
This actor, who played the bandit in Rashomon, appeared in many other Kurosawa films.

clues.clue: Said to be the first film to aim the camera directly at the sun
examples: This director is said to be the first to have aimed the camera directly at the sun, for a 1950 film which ends with the sun coming out as an abandoned baby is taken home by the woodcutter

works.name: Ran (1985)
works.description: Kurosawa's adaptation of King Lear, in which warlord Hidetora Ichimonji attempts to divide his kingdom among his three sons, with catastrophic results. Features a Toru Takemitsu score. The blind peasant Tsurumaru is the only one to escape the carnage. Ends with a blood-orange sky behind blind Tsurumaru atop ruins. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Adaptation of King Lear; warlord Hidetora Ichimonji divides his kingdom among his three sons with disastrous results
examples: an adaptation of King Lear in which a warlord of the Ichimonji clan attempts to divide his land among his three sons, with obviously disastrous results
Known for retelling the King Lear story in Ran

examples: The blind peasant Tsurumaru is the only one to escape the carnage of this film.

works.name: Ikiru (1952)
works.description: Film about a bureaucrat (inspired by Tolstoy's The Death of Ivan Ilyich) who is secretly diagnosed with stomach cancer and told he has six months to live. He devotes his remaining time to building a playground in a slum. Ends with him singing 'The Gondola Song' on a snowy swing set. Features Kurosawa's trademark wipe transitions. Wikipedia

clues.clue: A bureaucrat diagnosed with stomach cancer (told it's a 'mild ulcer') devotes his remaining life to building a children's playground in a slum
examples: This Kurosawa film details the story of an old bureaucrat who, upon learning he has stomach cancer, decides to build a children's playground.
a bureaucrat is diagnosed with a 'mild ulcer' by a doctor who secretly knows that the bureaucrat actually has stomach cancer and likely has six months to live

clues.clue: The protagonist was inspired by Ivan Ilyich from Tolstoy's novella The Death of Ivan Ilyich
examples: A character in one of this director's films who was inspired by Ivan Ilyich builds a playground in a slum

clues.clue: Ends with the protagonist singing 'The Gondola Song' on a snowy swing set

clues.clue: The film begins with a series of shots depicting parents being thwarted in attempts to have a playground built
examples: This film begins with a series of shots depicting a concerned group of parents being thwarted in their attempts to convince their city to build a playground.

clues.clue: Features Kurosawa's trademark horizontal wipe transitions linking a series of bureaucrats who pass women around in a circular referral process
examples: These transitions link a series of bureaucrats who refer a group of women to other departments in a circular process in Kurosawa's Ikiru. Star Wars also borrows Kurosawa's trademark use of the 'horizontal' type of this transition.

works.name: High and Low (1963)
works.description: Crime thriller adapted from Ed McBain's novel King's Ransom, in which shoe company executive Kingo Gondo discovers that kidnappers have accidentally abducted his chauffeur's son instead of his own. Features a memorable climactic train sequence and a confrontation scene through reflective glass/wire mesh. Wikipedia

examples: In another film by a director with this surname, kidnappers mistakenly abduct a chauffeur's child for ransom.
Mifune starred as shoe company executive Kingo Gondo in this adaptation of Ed McBain's novel King's Ransom. In this film, the son of Gondo's chauffeur is kidnapped instead of Gondo's real son.

clues.clue: Final confrontation scene: protagonist and villain separated by wire mesh and reflective glass, their faces superimposing when either speaks
examples: A film by this director ends with the protagonist and villain separated by wire mesh and a glass so harshly reflective, that when one of them speaks, the face of the other perfectly superimposes him.

works.name: Yojimbo (1961)
works.description: Film about a bodyguard (played by Toshiro Mifune) who manipulates two rival criminal families into fighting each other. Inspired by Dashiell Hammett's Red Harvest. Remade without permission as Sergio Leone's A Fistful of Dollars (Kurosawa sued and won) and also as Django (1966). Sanjuro was its sequel. Wikipedia

examples: Red Harvest inspired his film about a bodyguard pitting rival gangs against each other that was remade into A Fist Full of Dollars.
a film about two criminal families competing for the services of a bodyguard

clues.clue: Remade without permission as Sergio Leone's A Fistful of Dollars (starring Clint Eastwood); Kurosawa's company sued Leone and won
examples: This director remarked that an unlicensed movie based on one of his films was 'a fine movie, but it was my movie.' ...his company sued Sergio Leone for his film A Fistful of Dollars, which completely ripped off one of this man's movies.

works.name: Drunken Angel (1948)
works.description: Kurosawa's first major film and his first of sixteen collaborations with Toshiro Mifune. An alcoholic doctor bonds with a yakuza gangster who has tuberculosis. Kurosawa used his score strategically to get the film past censors. Wikipedia

examples: This director of Dreams relied on his score to thwart censors in a film about a bond between a tubercular lowlife and his alcoholic doctor, the titular Drunken Angel.

works.name: Dreams (1990)
works.description: Anthology film composed of eight dream segments, including 'The Peach Orchard' and 'Crows.' In 'Crows,' an aspiring artist enters a Van Gogh landscape and meets a Martin Scorsese-portrayed Van Gogh. Kurosawa's autobiography recounts how a bathtub sense-memory influenced the Chopin/Van Gogh segment. Wikipedia

works.name: Dersu Uzala (1975)
works.description: Kurosawa's only 70mm film, made for Mosfilm (Soviet studio). Follows the Siberian hunter Dersu Uzala. Features a famous shot in which the moon and setting sun are visible in opposite corners of the frame while Dersu explains that the sun is 'the most important person.' Wikipedia

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Akira Kurosawa (1910–1998) was a Japanese filmmaker considered one of the three great directors of Golden Age Japanese cinema, alongside Yasujiro Ozu and Kenji Mizoguchi. He had a 16-film collaboration with actor Toshiro Mifune. He wrote the memoir Something Like an Autobiography. His films profoundly influenced Western cinema, particularly George Lucas and Sergio Leone. Kurosawa is known for his trademark use of horizontal wipe transitions. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Long-term collaborator with actor Toshiro Mifune across 16 films
examples: often worked with Toshiro Mifune
An alcoholic doctor diagnoses a gangster with tuberculosis in the first of this director's sixteen films with a certain actor.

clues.clue: Considered one of the three great directors of Golden Age Japanese cinema alongside Yasujiro Ozu and Kenji Mizoguchi
examples: Mizoguchi is generally considered one of the three great directors of Golden Age Japanese cinema, alongside Yasujiro Ozu and this director of Seven Samurai and Rashomon.

clues.clue: Star Wars (1977) by George Lucas was influenced by Kurosawa's films; Lucas used his influence to fund Kagemusha; Lucas described Kurosawa's attention to detail with the term 'immaculate reality'
examples: Part of the influence of silent film and the Western genre on one of this director's 1977 breakout film comes filtered through the films of the other director, like Throne of Blood and Ran.
In one interview, one of these directors described the influence of the other on his attention to detail and unglamorous nature of set design with the term 'immaculate reality'

examples: After the producers refused the demands of their first choice, Akira Kurosawa, Kon Ichikawa directed a 1965 film about this sort of event

clues.clue: Included a character named Murakami in his crime drama Stray Dog
examples: This earlier Japanese director included a character called Murakami in his crime drama Stray Dog. His later films include Rashomon and Seven Samurai.

comprehensive_summary:
Akira Kurosawa was a Japanese film director whose career spanned from the 1940s through the 1990s. He is considered one of the three great directors of the Golden Age of Japanese cinema, alongside Yasujiro Ozu and Kenji Mizoguchi. His most frequently clued film is Seven Samurai (1954), in which a group of peasants hire seven samurai to defend their village from bandits; the film ends with two samurai dying to musket fire in a rainy battle as a survivor remarks 'The farmers have won, not us.' The Magnificent Seven (1960) by John Sturges was a Western remake starring Yul Brynner. George Lucas adapted the two bickering peasant framing characters from Seven Samurai into C-3PO and R2-D2 for Star Wars.

Kurosawa's Rashomon (1950) presents multiple contradictory testimonies about a samurai's murder and his wife's rape; it ends with a woodcutter taking in an abandoned baby. Toshiro Mifune played the bandit Tajomaru. This film is said to be the first to aim the camera directly at the sun. Ran (1985) adapts Shakespeare's King Lear: warlord Hidetora Ichimonji divides his kingdom among three sons with disastrous results. The blind peasant Tsurumaru is the only one to escape the carnage; the film ends with a blood-orange sky behind a blind man on ruins. The court jester Kyoami appears. Toru Takemitsu's score includes 'Flute of Darkness' and 'Hell's Picture Scroll.'

Ikiru (1952) follows a bureaucrat secretly diagnosed with stomach cancer (told he has a 'mild ulcer') who devotes his remaining months to building a playground in a slum. Inspired by Tolstoy's The Death of Ivan Ilyich, the film ends with the protagonist singing 'The Gondola Song' on a snowy swing set. Kurosawa's trademark horizontal wipe transitions appear in Ikiru, linking bureaucrats who shuffle women between departments. High and Low (1963), adapted from Ed McBain's King's Ransom, stars Toshiro Mifune as shoe executive Kingo Gondo, whose chauffeur's child is accidentally kidnapped instead of Gondo's own son. The film's climax features a rigged briefcase thrown from a bathroom window during a train sequence; a single shot of pink smoke is the only color in the otherwise black-and-white finale.

Yojimbo (1961) features a bodyguard pitting two criminal families against each other, inspired by Dashiell Hammett's Red Harvest. Sergio Leone remade it without permission as A Fistful of Dollars; Kurosawa sued and called it 'a fine movie, but it was my movie.' Django (1966) starring Franco Nero was another unauthorized remake. Sanjuro (1962) was the sequel to Yojimbo. Throne of Blood (1957) adapts Macbeth for feudal Japan, with Mifune in the lead. Drunken Angel (1948) was Kurosawa's first of sixteen collaborations with Mifune; an alcoholic doctor bonds with a yakuza gangster who has tuberculosis, and Kurosawa used the score to evade censors. Kagemusha (1980) won the Palme d'Or and shows a thief impersonating the deceased warlord Shingen; George Lucas helped fund the film after Star Wars' success. Dreams (1990) is an anthology film including 'Crows,' in which an art student hears Chopin's Raindrop Prelude and enters Van Gogh's Wheatfield with Crows, meeting a Martin Scorsese-played Van Gogh. Dersu Uzala (1975), Kurosawa's only 70mm film, was made for the Soviet Mosfilm studio and follows Siberian hunter Dersu Uzala. Hidden Fortress (1958) uses two bickering peasants as framing characters, directly adapted by Lucas.

Kurosawa wrote the memoir Something Like an Autobiography, whose opening chapter traces a bathtub sense-memory that influenced the Dreams segment, and another chapter describes his crew's failed attempt to film on Mt. Fuji's summit. He was also the first choice to direct the 1964 Tokyo Olympics documentary but was replaced by Kon Ichikawa after a dispute with producers. His crime drama Stray Dog featured a character named Murakami. Lucas described Kurosawa's set design philosophy with the term 'immaculate reality.'
recursive_suggestions: Seven Samurai — highest frequency Kurosawa film; rich plot and character clues worth deeper search (Fine Arts)
Rashomon — second most frequently clued; landmark in film history (Fine Arts)
Ikiru — multiple specific clues; Ivan Ilyich connection; swing set ending (Fine Arts)
Ran — many detail clues; Takemitsu score, Kyoami, King Lear adaptation (Fine Arts)
High and Low — detailed plot mechanic clues; train sequence, reflective glass confrontation (Fine Arts)
Yojimbo — frequently clued via Leone/Django connection; specific plot clues (Fine Arts)
Toshiro Mifune — long-term collaborator clued repeatedly across many Kurosawa films (Fine Arts)

Film: protagonist's character arc inspired by Ivan Ilyich from Tolstoy's The Death of Ivan Ilyich

Film: also remade without permission as Django (1966), starring Franco Nero as a former Union soldier who carries a coffin containing a machine gun
Film: Macbeth adaptation set in feudal Japan; Toshiro Mifune plays the Macbeth analogue; influenced George Lucas alongside Ran
Film: first of Kurosawa's sixteen collaborations with Toshiro Mifune; an alcoholic doctor bonds with a yakuza gangster who has tuberculosis; Kurosawa used the score to evade censors
